description: DNS resolves names to IP addresses and can reveal domains, subdomains, mail servers, name servers, internal hostnames, and sometimes full zone data when misconfigured.
why_prioritize: DNS can expand your target list by revealing hostnames, subdomains, and infrastructure clues.
Misconfigured DNS servers may allow zone transfers, which can expose many records at once.
DNS findings often help connect web, mail, SMB, and database services to real hostnames.

enumeration_steps.title: Identify basic DNS records
enumeration_steps.reason: Basic records help you understand the domain structure and important hosts.

enumeration_steps.title: Check for zone transfer
enumeration_steps.reason: Zone transfer can expose a full list of DNS records when the server is misconfigured.

enumeration_steps.title: Run DNS enumeration tools
enumeration_steps.reason: DNS enumeration tools can automate common checks and organize useful records.

enumeration_steps.title: Check reverse DNS when you have IP ranges
enumeration_steps.reason: Reverse DNS records can reveal hostnames that are not obvious from the main website or scan output.

next_steps: Save the full zone output for review.
Extract hostnames and add them to your notes.
Map discovered hostnames to IP addresses and services.
Use the hostnames for web virtual host checks and targeted enumeration.
Resolve each subdomain and check whether it points to an in-scope IP.
Browse web-related subdomains manually.
Run focused scans against newly discovered in-scope hosts when allowed.
Look for names like dev, test, staging, admin, backup, git, vpn, mail, or db.
Check whether SMTP or webmail services are exposed.
Note email domain patterns that may reveal username formats.
Use mail hostnames as leads for service enumeration.
Look for SPF, DKIM, and DMARC TXT records that reveal infrastructure.
Record the naming pattern and reuse it when guessing related hosts in labs.
Check whether names reveal roles such as db, backup, intranet, files, dc, or admin.
Use discovered hostnames when testing virtual hosts on web servers.
Connect hostnames to services found in your Nmap results.
Review the TXT content for service names, verification tokens, cloud providers, or email security records.
Check whether any values reveal internal domains or third-party services.
Use discovered domains or providers as context for later enumeration.
Do not treat tokens as credentials unless the lab clearly makes them useful.
Record the DNS software and version.
Search for known issues affecting that exact version.
Confirm the version with more than one method when possible.
Continue record enumeration before assuming exploitability.

beginner_notes: DNS enumeration needs a domain name. If you only have an IP, look for hostnames in Nmap output, web pages, certificates, or reverse DNS.
Use discovered hostnames as leads for virtual host testing on web services.
Zone transfer is a high-value finding in labs, but most real systems block it.
Keep DNS findings organized because they often connect many services together.
